title: Effectively Using an AI Coding Assistant
author: Chris Warren
text:
Effectively Using an AI Coding Assistant
Recently, I’ve been working on a personal coding project. Beyond the product itself, I’ve been using the project as a deliberate learning exercise in working with an AI coding assistant. Not just for throwaway prototypes or vibe coding sessions, but for building production-viable systems where correctness, structure, and maintainability matter.
This isn’t meant to be a comprehensive guide. Instead, it’s a collection of ideas, techniques, and caveats I’ve picked up along the way using Copilot in VSCode. My hope is that they’ll be useful to other developers trying to move past surface-level AI usage and integrate these tools into serious development work.
Preparing for an AI-Assisted Development Session
One habit I’ve found particularly valuable is keeping a dedicated document alongside my project where I manage development prompts. For me, that’s a Google Doc, but the tool doesn’t matter as much as the practice. This document serves as a workspace for crafting complex prompts, recording follow-ups during reviews, and capturing ideas without derailing the current coding flow. It’s essentially a rich scratchpad, but I’ve found that giving it some structure makes a big difference.
In this document, I separate work into major sections by task or story. Within each task, I keep related prompt chains together so I can track how a solution evolves. Usually, I write the initial prompt in this doc, then copy it into the AI chat interface to kick off the conversation. As the AI responds and we iterate through the chat interface, I make notes about issues I notice or ideas for follow-up prompts. This keeps the workflow focused and prevents those “I should come back to this later” thoughts from getting lost.
I also use this document to help spin up new chats when needed. AI sessions don’t retain long-term context well, so when I need to start fresh, having a quick reference makes it easier to get the AI back up to speed. I keep a summary of the project, notes about my development environment (like OS details, custom utilities, and deployment flow), and a brief history of the work to date. Having all of that in one place lowers friction when I need to reframe the AI’s understanding.
The Importance of Validation
Automation is the fastest way to make mistakes at speed, and AI magnifies that. One of the biggest shifts when working with AI is the speed at which new functionality gets generated. That creates a risk of pushing forward without fully validating the changes. To avoid this, I’ve made validation a central part of my workflow, not just something that happens at release time.
Making it a part of my workflow means that it needs to be non-disruptive — fast, easy, and with a low transition cost. For me, validation usually combines automated tests, manual state inspection after deployment (often in the AWS console), and direct interaction with the application itself. My app’s status page includes some deeper checks than a simple ‘is alive’, and for some features I’ll make a dedicated validation interface. Simply poking at the new feature also can’t be disregarded. The AI typically can’t perform this validation directly, but it’s very good at helping you create tailored validation plans, which helps lower the friction shifting to validation. I regularly prompt it to generate ad hoc playbooks or test checklists based on the specific feature I’m building.
Working with AI as a Coding Partner
I find it helpful to think of the AI as a junior coding partner — one who is extraordinarily knowledgeable in some ways but lacks judgment and broader context. It’s like working with a bootcamp grad who knows every API and library but still needs guidance on architectural decisions and good practices. To use home construction as an analogy, the AI often chooses great materials for the job, but without oversight it builds walls in inconsistent, sub-optimal ways or places key components where they’ll create maintenance headaches later. For example, it might build two bathrooms on separate floors but fail to align them vertically, making the plumbing far more complicated than it needs to be.
Fortunately, the AI responds well to feedback. When I point out problems it quickly adapts, either picking a better path itself, or following specific guidance when provided. This is especially true for things like naming conventions. I find that about 80 percent of the time, the AI generates solid names for variables and functions. The other 20 percent are either too generic or actively misleading. Asking the AI to refactor names usually works well, and it’s often worth explicitly prompting it to review and improve its naming choices for clarity.
Strengths and Weaknesses in Practice
The AI shines when generating specific code or scaffolding out new files. It’s also pretty good at following established project structures once those structures are in place. However, it struggles with mid-level organization. Within a file, it often places code in a way that isn’t ideal for developer comprehension. Across files, it sometimes puts new functions in the wrong place entirely. I usually handle in-file organization manually, setting up patterns with clear comments or region markers that the AI can follow later. For cross-file refactoring, I often need to give the AI a follow-up prompt to move code to the correct location or extract it into a new module.
Another challenge is the AI’s tendency to lose track of longer-term context. It’s not always obvious when this happens, so I’ve learned to watch for subtle signs that the AI is working from incomplete or outdated assumptions. Keeping good notes and being ready to reestablish context is part of the job.
Slinging Code
AI-generated code can be fast, but fast code generation also means fast error propagation if you’re not careful. I’ve gotten comfortable with committing changes frequently, even when the code isn’t in a working state. Adding tags or prefixes to these commits helps me keep track of exploratory or intermediate work. When things go sideways, I often find it faster to revert and re-prompt than to coax the AI into patching up half-broken code.
Another useful habit is to focus on outcomes rather than specific implementations. It’s tempting to give the AI detailed instructions about how to do something, but often it’s better to describe the desired result, define the constraints, and let the AI propose a solution. Sometimes the AI comes up with approaches I hadn’t considered. That said, it’s still important to understand the “how” well enough to know what constraints to set, to review the code, and to provide course corrections. When the AI makes larger changes, I usually ask for explanations of its reasoning. Often, I find that its choices make sense given certain assumptions, but those assumptions are incorrect or incomplete. Identifying and addressing those gaps early saves time.
For larger chunks of work, I may ask the AI to generate an implementation plan before touching the code. Reviewing and refining that plan gives me a chance to call out specific concerns in advance. For example, I might tell it to avoid a certain pattern, prioritize fault tolerance in a specific area, or ensure that logging is rich enough for observability but avoids sensitive information. This kind of process management helps separate production-oriented coding from casual prototyping. You’re still delegating a lot of work to the AI, but you’re staying in control of structure and quality.
Templates, Patterns, and Standardization
AI coding assistants are especially good at following examples. I’ve found it worth the time to create polished versions of common tasks, both at the code level and at the workflow level. For instance, I have a standard sequence for adding a new section to the site, and I keep that pattern handy to guide the AI when similar tasks come up. I also maintain a reference document that covers development practices and project standards. Sometimes the AI picks these things up automatically, but not always. Rather than assume it will, I make a habit of referring to these templates or pasting in guidelines explicitly in my prompts.
Interestingly, the AI is capable of helping with this scaffolding work too. I’ve asked it to generate initial versions of templates and development flow guides, then polished them afterward. This creates a feedback loop where the AI contributes not just to code but to process improvement.
Choosing the Right Task Size
One of the hardest things to calibrate is the size of the work you ask the AI to handle in a single prompt. The AI is happy to make large sweeping changes, but that can leave you with a tangle of updates that are hard to track and validate. This might be fine for hobby projects or vibe coding, but it’s a real problem when you’re building something robust.
I think of prompt sizing the same way I think about writing tasks for human developers. Small, verifiable increments work best. For me, this usually means tasks that would land around a two or three in story points. Sometimes I’ll push to a five, but if a task feels like an eight, it’s too big and needs to be broken down. On the other hand, if something feels like a one, the AI can usually handle it instantly — I’ll just fold that change into whatever other work I’m doing.
At times, I deliberately use high-level prompts to explore different implementation paths. For example, I might ask the AI to “add a user account system” and then follow up by asking why it chose the approach it did, what alternatives it considered, and what tradeoffs it sees. After that exploration, I’ll reset the codebase and move forward with smaller, more deliberate tasks for the real implementation.
Tips and Caveats
Here are a few recurring lessons I’ve picked up that are worth noting.
- Rich development logging is invaluable. The AI is good at adding logging when prompted, and I’ve found it useful to ask for consistent labels that make it easier to find and strip out temporary logs later.
- The AI handles copy-pasted info from the AWS console surprisingly well. This is useful because the AIs assumptions about what ought to be (e.g. after a terraform apply) can diverge significantly from reality, and being able to quickly tell it “this is what’s actually there” is very useful for correcting those assumptions and for debugging some things.
- It has a very strong bias towards people using a linux shell. I do that much of the time, but in this case I’m often using Windows Powershell. When it recommends commands to run it almost invariably assumes linux (e.g. chaining commands via &&) no matter how often I tell it otherwise. However, it is good at providing the Powershell version when explicitly asked on a case-by-case basis.
- When you ask the AI a question, it often treats it as a prompt to make changes rather than simply an inquiry. This can lead to unintended modifications if you’re not careful.
- The AI tends to mix in functional changes with refactoring, even when asked not to. The best way to avoid this is to work in small steps, be clear about intent, and always review the output closely.
- Outdated knowledge is also a real issue, especially when working with constantly evolving services like AWS. If you find yourself stuck on a problem that feels harder than it should, it’s often because the AI is working with deprecated paths or incomplete information. In those cases, checking current documentation and nudging the AI to reset its assumptions can get things back on track.
- The AI tends to understate its confidence. When I ask why it did something, it often responds with, “You’re right, that’s not the best approach,” and immediately offers a revision, even when its initial decision was reasonable. It’s important to recognize this pattern and push for real explanations, not just reflexive agreement. Be explicit about “I don’t like your approach, do this instead” vs “I don’t understand your approach, explain it” vs “this other approach may be better, but maybe not — explain why you chose A instead of B”.
- Refactoring a function location often misses a few references. I find I have to do a secondary prompt to tell it to check and fix all the imports for the function (or just do it manually).
Closing Thoughts
Working with an AI coding assistant is making me a better engineer and engineering manager. That’s not because of the specific code the AI writes, but because of the way it forces me to think carefully about specifications, review implementations, and guide the refinement process. The work is faster, more iterative, and often more fun, but it still requires deliberate attention to quality, structure, and validation.
